article_title: Leveraging Rust and the GPU to render user interfaces at 120 FPS — Zed's Blog
core_thesis: Zed's UI framework, GPUI, achieves high-performance rendering (120 FPS) by leveraging Rust and the GPU, drawing inspiration from video game rendering techniques. Instead of using a general-purpose graphics library, GPUI uses custom shaders for specific UI primitives (rectangles, shadows, text, icons, images) and employs signed distance functions (SDFs), closed-form blur approximations, and glyph atlases to minimize CPU overhead and maximize GPU parallelism.
detailed_summary: The article explains how Zed, a code editor built in Rust, uses its own UI framework called GPUI to render at 120 FPS. The author, Antonio Scandurra, contrasts the performance struggles of Electron-based apps (like Atom) with the consistent frame rates of video games, motivating the decision to build a GPU-driven UI. GPUI focuses on a small set of primitives: rectangles, drop shadows, text, icons, and images. For rectangles, GPUI uses signed distance functions (SDFs) in the fragment shader to draw rounded rectangles efficiently. Drop shadows are rendered using a technique by Evan Wallace (Figma co-founder) that approximates a Gaussian blur with a closed-form solution for straight rectangles and a hybrid approach for rounded rectangles. Text rendering uses OS-level shaping (e.g., CoreText) and rasterization, caching glyphs in a GPU atlas with only alpha channels to allow tinting. Icons and images follow a similar atlas approach. The framework abstracts these low-level details behind an `Element` trait with `layout` and `paint` methods, inspired by Flutter's constraint-based layout. The `Scene` struct collects primitives into layers, and the renderer draws them in a fixed order (shadows, rectangles, glyphs, etc.), with support for stacking contexts via layers.

entities.name: Antonio Scandurra
entities.role: Author of the blog post and likely a developer at Zed Industries.

entities.name: Zed
entities.role: The code editor being developed, using GPUI for rendering.

entities.name: GPUI
entities.role: Zed's custom UI framework that renders using Rust and GPU shaders.

entities.name: Evan Wallace
entities.role: Co-founder of Figma; his technique for drop shadows is used in GPUI.

entities.name: Inigo Quilez
entities.role: Known for seminal work on signed distance functions, referenced for SDF techniques.

entities.name: Flutter
entities.role: UI framework that inspired GPUI's layout model.

entities.name: CoreText
entities.role: macOS text shaping and rasterization API used by GPUI.

entities.name: Metal Shader Language
entities.role: GPU shading language used to write GPUI's shaders.

key_data_points: A modern display's refresh rate is 60-120 FPS, giving only 8.33ms per frame for all rendering work. (🟢 Relevant to performance-critical applications: understanding this constraint is key for building responsive UIs.)

GPUI uses signed distance functions (SDFs) in fragment shaders to draw rounded rectangles, allowing GPU-parallelized rendering. (🟢 Relevant to graphics programming: SDFs are a powerful technique for 2D/3D rendering.)

Drop shadows are rendered using a closed-form approximation of Gaussian blur for straight rectangles, and a hybrid method for rounded rectangles that performs a 4-step manual convolution along one axis. (🟢 Relevant to UI development: efficient shadow rendering without expensive blur passes.)
Text shaping is done via OS APIs (e.g., CoreText) and cached per text-font pair; only changed text is reshaped per frame. (🟢 Relevant to text-heavy applications: caching avoids redundant shaping.)

Glyphs are rasterized on the CPU (using OS rasterizer) with only alpha channel, stored in a GPU atlas, and tinted via shader multiplication. Up to 16 variants per glyph are stored for sub-pixel positioning. (🟢 Relevant to performance: atlas reduces draw calls; alpha-only storage saves memory.)

The `Element` trait in GPUI separates layout and painting, with constraints flowing down and sizes flowing up, inspired by Flutter. (🟢 Relevant to UI framework design: this pattern enables composable and efficient layout.)

The `Scene` struct collects primitives into layers; rendering order is fixed (shadows, rectangles, glyphs, etc.) but layers can be pushed for z-ordering. (🟢 Relevant to rendering pipelines: understanding draw order prevents visual artifacts.)
methodology_steps: Define a rectangle SDF in the fragment shader: center the rectangle at origin, use absolute value for symmetry, compute distance to the nearest edge or corner, and subtract corner radius for rounded corners.

Implement drop shadows using Evan Wallace's technique: for straight rectangles, use the error function (erf) for a closed-form Gaussian blur; for rounded rectangles, perform a 4-step manual convolution along one axis while using the closed-form along the other.

For text rendering: shape text using OS APIs (e.g., CoreText), cache shaped glyphs per text-font pair, rasterize glyphs on CPU with only alpha channel (16 sub-pixel variants), pack into a GPU atlas using bin-packing (etagere), and render via a single instanced draw call that maps target position to atlas position.
For icons and images: rasterize SVG icons on CPU (alpha only for tinting), upload images with full color to a separate texture, and render using similar instanced draw calls as glyphs.

Implement the `Element` trait with `layout` and `paint` methods: layout receives a `SizeConstraint` (min/max) and returns the element's size; paint pushes primitives (rectangles, glyphs, etc.) to a `Scene` and positions children accordingly.
Use layers for z-ordering: elements can push a new `Layer` to the scene to ensure their primitives render on top of parent primitives.
conclusions: GPUI achieves high frame rates by focusing on a small set of UI primitives and using custom GPU shaders, avoiding the overhead of general-purpose graphics libraries. This approach is validated by Zed's ability to render at 120 FPS consistently.

The use of signed distance functions for rectangles and closed-form blur approximations for shadows demonstrates that mathematical optimizations can replace expensive multi-pass rendering, making GPU-based UI feasible for text-heavy applications.
Caching shaped text and rasterized glyphs in a GPU atlas, combined with instanced draw calls, minimizes CPU-GPU data transfer and allows text rendering to approach GPU bandwidth limits.

The separation of layout and painting via the `Element` trait, inspired by Flutter, provides a composable and efficient way to build complex UIs while keeping the rendering pipeline simple and predictable.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your interests in programming, homelabbing, and DevOps because it provides a deep dive into GPU-accelerated UI rendering using Rust, a systems programming language you likely appreciate. The techniques described—SDFs, GPU atlases, and instanced rendering—are directly applicable to building high-performance graphical tools, which could be useful for developing custom dashboards, monitoring interfaces, or even game-like visualizations for your homelab. As a programmer, you can learn how to leverage Rust and GPU shaders (Metal Shader Language) to offload rendering work, a skill transferable to other graphics APIs like Vulkan or WebGPU. The article also touches on caching strategies and layout algorithms that are valuable for optimizing any performance-critical application.
extra_advice: Consider experimenting with GPUI's concepts in your own projects: try implementing a simple SDF-based rectangle renderer using WebGPU or Vulkan in Rust. This will solidify your understanding of GPU programming and could lead to building a custom UI for a homelab monitoring tool. Additionally, explore the `etagere` crate for bin-packing if you ever need to manage texture atlases.
